q-voilewp-voilealluremarineleventarriรจr.stem: What is observed on a running point of sail (dead downwind)?
choices: Rolling increases and an (accidental) gybe threatens
Speed becomes maximal, higher than on a broad reach
The sailboat can no longer make headway because it is head to wind
q-voilewp-voilealluremarineleventarriรจr.explanation: On a run the airflow over the sails is disturbed: speed drops compared with a broad reach, rolling increases and an accidental gybe threatens.

q-voilewp-voilealluremarineleventdebout-.stem: On the head-to-wind point of sail, what must the sailboat do to make headway under sail?
choices: Bear away about 45ยฐ from the wind axis (30ยฐ on the highest-performance racing yachts)
Hold its course exactly head to wind, sheeting its sails in fully
Set the spinnaker to catch the wind coming from ahead
q-voilewp-voilealluremarineleventdebout-.explanation: Head to wind the sailboat cannot make headway: it must bear away about 45ยฐ from the wind axis (30ยฐ in racing) and beat to windward (tack).

q-voilewp-voilealluremarinetribordamures.stem: When is a sailboat said to be ยซon starboard tackยป?
choices: When the wind comes from the right relative to the boat's axis
When it sails downwind with the sails wing-on-wing (in scissors)
When it has right of way over every other boat on the water
q-voilewp-voilealluremarinetribordamures.explanation: A sailboat is on starboard tack when the wind comes from the right (starboard), and on port tack in the opposite case. These distinctions underlie the right-of-way rules between sailboats.

q-voilewp-voilechaviragesourceduphรฉnomร.stem: On a light sport dinghy, what is a capsize commonly called and what is its nature?
choices: A ยซcapsizeยป: it is common and harmless
A ยซshipwreckยป: it almost always leads to the loss of the boat
A ยซrounding upยป: it occurs only when close-hauled
q-voilewp-voilechaviragesourceduphรฉnomร.explanation: On a light dinghy the capsize is common and harmless; it results from seeking excessive speed or from poor crew placement.

q-voilewp-voileempannagedรฉfinition-34dc.stem: What characterises the gybe (empannage) compared with tacking?
choices: The change of tack is made by passing through the wind astern (downwind)
The change of tack is made by passing head to wind
It does not change the set of the sails
q-voilewp-voileempannagedรฉfinition-34dc.explanation: A gybe changes tack by passing through the wind astern (downwind); passing head to wind is instead a tack (virement de bord).

q-voilewp-voilefocprรฉsentation-30eec01b.stem: What is the jib on a sailboat?
choices: A triangular headsail, held to the forestay (stay) by its luff
The mainsail, rigged abaft the mast
A standing-rigging wire that holds the mast up
q-voilewp-voilefocprรฉsentation-30eec01b.explanation: The jib is a triangular headsail held to the stay by its luff; it plays a key role in the success of the tack.

q-voilewp-voilevirementdebordprincipe-90.stem: What happens during a tack (virement de bord)?
choices: The side of the boat that was leeward becomes windward, and vice versa
The sailboat keeps the same tack throughout the manoeuvre
The manoeuvre is necessarily made by passing through the wind astern
q-voilewp-voilevirementdebordprincipe-90.explanation: In a tack, the side that was leeward becomes windward (a change of tack made by passing head to wind).
